title: Local rules: read the scorecard's fine print
microFact: Every course adds its own local rules — they're printed on the scorecard, and they change what you can do.
lesson:
Beyond the universal Rules, each club posts LOCAL RULES on the scorecard or a noticeboard. Common ones in India:

• PREFERRED LIES ("winter rules"): during rough turf conditions, you may lift, clean and place the ball within a scorecard's length in the fairway. Only when declared — not a standing right.
• GROUND UNDER REPAIR (GUR): white-lined areas or marked zones — free relief, and sometimes play is prohibited from them.
• Staked TREES / flower beds: often mandatory free relief.
• INTERNAL OB: white stakes between certain holes to stop shortcut lines.
• Stroke-and-distance alternative (drop near the loss point for 2 strokes) for casual play.
• ANIMAL scrapes and monkey/dog interference — genuinely common at Indian courses; a moved ball at rest gets replaced without penalty if an animal moved it.

First-tee fluency move: flip the scorecard over and scan the local rules while others warm up. Ten seconds, and you'll be the one answering questions mid-round instead of asking them.

quiz.question: A dog runs off with your ball at rest in the fairway (it happens in India). What's the ruling?
options: Play a new ball with a one-stroke penalty
Replace a ball on the original spot, no penalty
The ball must be played where the dog drops it
quiz.explanation: An outside influence moving your ball at rest = replace it where it was, penalty-free. Estimate the spot if needed.
